refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature database) and can be queried by downstream planning and execution functions to retrieve spatial attributes (position, geometry, classification) for an agreed set of site features.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and restricted areas in the spatial model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and provide access to the worksite spatial model for planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign zone-level feature inventories (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; maintain zone-feature mappings in a relational schema; provide zone-based query API.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a voxel grid with occupancy and semantic labels; support spatial queries (collision detection, path feasibility) directly on grid; export grid snapshots for planning modules.

DP4: Integrate external geospatial data sources (GIS, CAD, site surveys) with real-time sensor observations; reconcile conflicts via confidence scoring; maintain dual representations (static reference + dynamic updates) with versioning.

DP5: Represent worksite as a topological graph of navigable regions and feature landmarks; encode safety constraints as edge/node attributes; support graph-based planning queries (reachability, safe routes).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

potential_sources: Sensor fusion logic tightly coupled to feature classification—mitigate by isolating classification rules into a separate policy module.
Coordinate frame assumptions (origin, orientation, units) must be agreed with planning/execution consumers—document as explicit interface contract.
Query interface design must remain agnostic to downstream consumer algorithms—use declarative query semantics (e.g., spatial predicates) rather than imperative traversal.
Safety constraint representation must align with operational policies—risk: policy changes require model schema updates; mitigate via attribute-based tagging rather than hard-coded rules.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 achieves functional independence by separating acquisition (sensing), fusion (data integration), representation (graph structure), and exposure (query API) as logical concerns. Coupling risks are primarily at boundaries (sensor assumptions, policy alignment, consumer expectations) rather than within the DP logic itself. Structured spatial graph is solution-neutral: can be realized as RDF, property graphs, or relational schemas.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Multi-modal sensing is available and calibrated; RISK: Sensor failures or miscalibration degrade model accuracy; MITIGATION: Implement sensor health monitoring and confidence scoring.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite features are static or change slowly relative to sensing cadence; RISK: Dynamic obstacles (moving equipment, personnel) may not be captured; MITIGATION: Define refresh rates and real-time update mechanisms.

ASSUMPTION: Downstream planning/execution functions can consume spatial graph queries; RISK: Consumer APIs may expect different representations (e.g., occupancy grids, topological maps); MITIGATION: Provide multiple export formats or adapter modules.
ASSUMPTION: Safety constraints are well-defined and stable; RISK: Operational policies may evolve; MITIGATION: Use attribute-based tagging to decouple constraint logic from model structure.

ASSUMPTION: Coordinate frame is globally consistent across all sensors and consumers; RISK: Frame drift or misalignment introduces systematic errors; MITIGATION: Implement frame validation and periodic recalibration.
ASSUMPTION: Feature classification taxonomy is agreed upon; RISK: Ambiguous or missing feature types cause model incompleteness; MITIGATION: Establish feature ontology and validation rules.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Required feature types and attributes (e.g., obstacles, hazards, access points, no-go zones); (2) Spatial accuracy and update frequency requirements; (3) Expected query patterns and latency constraints for planning/execution consumers; (4) Coordinate frame and units conventions; (5) Safety constraint representation format (e.g., attribute tags, policy rules, or geometric buffers). This will enable tighter DP-to-IM mapping and reduce ambiguity in module boundaries and interfaces.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial perception and fusion layer that acquires multi-modal sensor data, performs coordinate frame alignment, constructs and maintains a unified spatial graph with feature attributes, and exposes query interfaces for planning and execution consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound sensor streams (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) from Vehicle; raw spatial observations and ego-localization data.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound spatial graph queries and feature data served to planning/execution modules; responds to downstream requests for geometry, classification, and constraint metadata.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety constraint violations or anomalies detected during spatial fusion; alerts when feature attributes violate safety thresholds.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Optional: distributed sensor fusion or cloud-based spatial graph synchronization if multi-vehicle or remote perception is required.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for perception, sensor fusion, and spatial reasoning. Multi-modal sensing acquisition, coordinate frame unification, spatial graph construction, and feature attribute storage are core NGHE responsibilities. Query interface for downstream consumers aligns with NGHE's role as a perception backbone. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry inbound and Tasking/SafetyAlert outbound.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a hardware platform abstraction; it should not own spatial reasoning or graph storage. Sensor data acquisition belongs to Vehicle, but fusion logic, coordinate frame management, and query interfaces would diffuse significantly into planning and safety layers. Creates tight coupling between vehicle hardware and high-level reasoning.

Very poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop control interface, not a perception or fusion layer. Spatial graph storage and query logic have no cohesion with remote operator control. Would require extensive diffusion to planning and safety services.

Weak fit. Safety Services consumes spatial features to validate constraints, but should not own the spatial graph itself or multi-modal fusion logic. Conflates perception responsibility with safety validation. Spatial graph is a shared resource; centralizing it in Safety Services would create artificial coupling and reduce reusability by other planning/execution modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) multi-modal sensing fusion is a core perception function; (2) unified coordinate frame and spatial graph are canonical representations of the environment state; (3) query interface is a natural abstraction boundary for downstream consumers (planning, execution, safety); (4) cohesion is high—all elements (acquisition, fusion, storage, query) are tightly related perception tasks; (5) diffusion is minimal—only standard telemetry inbound and tasking/safety outbound.
containment_risks: If Vehicle module owns raw sensor drivers, a clear handoff interface (Telemetry) must be defined to avoid bidirectional coupling.
If multiple planning or safety modules query the spatial graph concurrently, query interface must be thread-safe and versioned to prevent race conditions.
Spatial graph storage may grow large; memory/persistence strategy must be defined within NGHE Solution scope to avoid diffusion to external storage layers.
If real-time latency is critical, fusion algorithm complexity must be managed within NGHE Solution to avoid pushing computation to Vehicle or Teleoperation layers.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly and completely addresses FR: multi-modal sensing acquisition + coordinate frame fusion + structured spatial graph storage + query interface collectively satisfy the requirement to 'capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.' DP is solution-complete and logically sound. No functional gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Vehicle module owns raw sensor drivers; Telemetry interface must enforce unidirectional handoff to prevent bidirectional coupling and diffusion of fusion logic into hardware abstraction layer.

Concurrent query access to spatial graph from multiple planning/execution/safety consumers requires thread-safety and versioning guarantees; if not enforced within NGHE Solution scope, diffusion risk to downstream modules managing their own graph copies.

Spatial graph persistence strategy (in-memory vs. disk vs. distributed) must be owned and managed entirely within NGHE Solution; deferring to external storage layers risks diffusion of graph semantics and versioning logic.

Real-time fusion latency constraints must be managed within NGHE Solution algorithm design; pushing computation burden to Vehicle or Teleoperation layers would violate separation of concerns and create artificial coupling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the optimal IM boundary with 0.95 containment score and 0.05 diffusion score. All core DP elements (acquisition, fusion, storage, query) are cohesively grouped within a single perception backbone module. External interfaces are clean and minimal: Telemetry (inbound sensor streams), Tasking (outbound spatial queries), SafetyAlert (outbound constraint violations), and optional Network Link (distributed sync). No significant diffusion detected. Recommended ownership is NGHE Solution.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion 'worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features' is directly verifiable: (1) spatial graph construction can be unit-tested with synthetic sensor data; (2) query interface can be integration-tested with planning/execution mock consumers; (3) feature coverage (geometry, classification, safety constraints) can be validated against worksite safety plan and operational policies. Traceability from FR → DP → IM is clear and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is unambiguously allocated as the owner of spatial perception, fusion, and graph management. Responsibilities are well-defined: acquire multi-modal telemetry, align coordinate frames, construct spatial graph, store feature attributes, expose query interface. Interfaces to Vehicle (Telemetry in), planning/execution (Tasking out), and Safety Services (SafetyAlert out) are explicit. No ambiguity in module boundaries or ownership.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Telemetry interface contract: specify sensor data schema, coordinate frame conventions, ego-localization format, and update frequency to prevent Vehicle module from diffusing fusion logic.

Define spatial graph query API specification: include query types (geometry lookup, classification filter, constraint check), response format, versioning strategy, and concurrency guarantees to ensure downstream consumers do not replicate graph logic.

Establish spatial graph persistence and memory management policy within NGHE Solution scope: define retention windows, update semantics, and consistency guarantees to prevent diffusion to external storage or caching layers.

Document real-time latency budget for fusion pipeline (sensor acquisition → coordinate alignment → graph update → query response) and allocate within NGHE Solution design to avoid pushing computation to Vehicle or Teleoperation.

Specify SafetyAlert interface: define which feature attribute violations trigger alerts, alert severity levels, and routing to Safety Services to ensure safety constraints are enforced at the perception layer without diffusing graph ownership.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence and clear traceability. However, the recommended_revisions identify critical interface and internal design contracts that must be decomposed into sub-FRs to ensure safety criticality is properly managed and diffusion risks are architecturally enforced. The spatial perception backbone is logically sound but requires next-level functional decomposition to address: (1) Telemetry interface contract enforcement, (2) spatial graph query API specification and concurrency guarantees, (3) persistence and memory management policy, (4) real-time latency budget allocation, and (5) SafetyAlert interface specification. These are not optional refinements—they are functional requirements that prevent diffusion and ensure safety constraints propagate correctly through the architecture. Decomposing now ensures each sub-FR has explicit ownership, verification criteria, and interface contracts before detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Acquire and normalize multi-modal sensor telemetry from Vehicle module via standardized Telemetry interface contract (sensor schema, coordinate frame conventions, ego-localization format, update frequency).
FR1.2: Fuse multi-modal sensor streams into a unified coordinate frame and maintain real-time ego-localization with latency budget ≤ [TBD] ms to support downstream planning/execution.

FR1.3: Construct and maintain a spatial graph data structure that stores worksite features (geometry, classification, safety attributes) with thread-safe concurrent query access and versioning guarantees.

FR1.4: Expose a spatial graph query API that supports geometry lookup, classification filtering, and constraint checking with defined response format, versioning strategy, and concurrency guarantees to prevent downstream replication of graph logic.

FR1.5: Manage spatial graph persistence (in-memory retention windows, update semantics, consistency guarantees) entirely within NGHE Solution scope without diffusion to external storage or caching layers.

FR1.6: Detect and alert Safety Services of feature attribute violations (geometry intrusions, classification anomalies, constraint breaches) via SafetyAlert interface with defined severity levels and routing logic.

revise.why: Current DP1 is solution-complete at the logical level but lacks explicit design parameters for interface contracts and internal architectural policies. Revise DP1 to include: (1) Telemetry interface schema and handoff protocol, (2) spatial graph query API specification with concurrency model, (3) persistence strategy and memory management policy, (4) real-time latency budget and fusion pipeline allocation, (5) SafetyAlert routing and severity classification. These revisions ensure DP1 is not only logically sound but also architecturally defensible against diffusion and safety-critical failures.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is the sole owner of spatial perception, fusion, and graph management; Vehicle module provides only raw sensor streams via Telemetry interface.
Downstream planning/execution/safety consumers access spatial graph exclusively through the query API; no direct graph mutation or replication is permitted.
Real-time fusion latency budget is a hard constraint that must be allocated and managed within NGHE Solution; no deferral to Vehicle or Teleoperation layers.
Spatial graph persistence is entirely managed within NGHE Solution; external storage layers (if used) are transparent to graph semantics and versioning.
SafetyAlert interface is the sole mechanism for propagating safety constraint violations; no safety logic is diffused to downstream consumers.
open_risks: Telemetry interface contract may be violated by Vehicle module if sensor schema or coordinate frame conventions are not formally specified and enforced; risk: fusion logic diffuses into hardware abstraction layer.

Concurrent query access to spatial graph may introduce race conditions or stale reads if versioning and thread-safety guarantees are not explicitly designed; risk: downstream consumers implement their own graph copies.
Real-time latency budget may be unachievable if fusion pipeline is not carefully allocated; risk: computation burden is pushed to Vehicle or Teleoperation layers, violating separation of concerns.
Spatial graph persistence strategy may be deferred to external storage layers without clear ownership; risk: graph semantics and versioning logic diffuse to storage layer.
SafetyAlert interface may be incomplete or ambiguous, leading to safety constraint violations being missed or misrouted; risk: safety criticality is compromised.
evidence_needed: Formal Telemetry interface specification: sensor data schema (format, units, coordinate frame), ego-localization format, update frequency, and error handling protocol.

Spatial graph query API specification: query types (geometry lookup, classification filter, constraint check), response format, versioning strategy (e.g., snapshot isolation, eventual consistency), and concurrency guarantees (e.g., thread-safe, lock-free).

Real-time latency budget allocation: end-to-end latency from sensor acquisition to query response, broken down by pipeline stage (acquisition, alignment, graph update, query execution), with justification for each stage.

Spatial graph persistence policy: retention windows, update semantics (e.g., append-only, in-place mutation), consistency guarantees (e.g., strong consistency, eventual consistency), and memory management strategy.

SafetyAlert interface specification: feature attribute violations that trigger alerts, alert severity levels (critical, warning, info), routing logic to Safety Services, and acceptance criteria for alert delivery.

Safety criticality analysis: mapping of safety constraints from worksite safety plan to spatial graph feature attributes and SafetyAlert triggers; verification that all safety-critical features are captured and monitored.
